quick_facts.label: Current status
quick_facts.value: LSAT—India appears to be discontinued rather than an active Indian law-admissions examination. A 2024 update states that Pearson VUE scrapped the examination, and current Jindal Global Law School (JGLS) five-year law admissions specify LNAT-UK and state that no other law-school admissions test is accepted.

quick_facts.label: Historical operator
quick_facts.value: For the 2024 cycle, LSAT—India was described as designed by the Law School Admission Council (LSAC) and delivered by Pearson VUE.

quick_facts.label: Last documented cycle
quick_facts.value: The last clearly documented LSAT—India cycle is 2024. Reporting on that cycle records a May 2024 registration window and acceptance by 18 law schools; these are historical facts, not a current admissions schedule or participating-college list.

quick_facts.label: Applicant action
quick_facts.value: Applicants seeking Indian undergraduate law admission should verify the entrance test directly with each target institution. For example, JGLS currently requires LNAT-UK for its five-year integrated B.A. LL.B., B.B.A. LL.B., and B.Com. LL.B. programmes, rather than LSAT—India.

sections.heading: What LSAT—India was—and its present status
paragraphs: LSAT—India was a law-school admissions test used by a group of Indian institutions. In its 2024 cycle, it was described as an LSAC-designed assessment delivered by Pearson VUE and accepted by 18 law schools. It was therefore not the common entrance test for all Indian law schools or National Law Universities; acceptance depended on the individual institution.

For current planning, treat LSAT—India as a legacy examination, not an open application route. A 2024 exam update says Pearson VUE scrapped it, while a current JGLS page for five-year integrated law programmes names LNAT-UK as the required test and expressly says JGLS does not accept another law-school admissions test. Prospective students should use each university's current admissions page rather than historical LSAT—India college lists.
bullets: Historical context: LSAT—India was used for admissions at participating institutions during the 2024 cycle.
Current implication: do not pay for preparation or assume a registration pathway exists for LSAT—India without an institution-specific, current official notice.
The international LSAT administered by LSAC is a separate test and should not be assumed to substitute for LSAT—India at an Indian university; follow the target university's stated test requirements.

sections.heading: Application guidance for Indian law aspirants
paragraphs: There is no current LSAT—India application process presented here because the available evidence identifies the examination as discontinued. Historical registration dates, fees, formats, and participating-college lists from 2024 should not be relied on for a future admission cycle.

Instead, begin with the admissions page of each target law school, identify its accepted examination(s), and apply through the school's official route. JGLS, for example, currently states that its five-year integrated law programmes use LNAT-UK, while its current postgraduate legal-studies requirements list JSAT–LAW 2026 or CLAT–PG 2026 for the LL.M. pathway. Requirements are programme-specific.
bullets: Check the exact programme, degree level, academic year, eligibility rules, test requirement, application deadline, and fee on the university's official page.

If you hold a historical LSAT—India score, ask the intended institution in writing whether it remains acceptable for the specific programme and intake; do not infer acceptance from an old participating-college list.
Use only official university admissions channels for applications and payments.

sections.heading: Admissions outcome and alternatives
paragraphs: Historically, an LSAT—India score could support an application only at schools that chose to accept it; it did not itself confer admission. The documented 2024 cohort included 18 accepting institutions, but that list was cycle-specific and has no demonstrated continuing validity after the examination's discontinuation.

The practical outcome for a present-day applicant is to select the test required by the desired institution. JGLS's current five-year integrated law admissions page identifies LNAT-UK as its route, whereas its current LL.M. admissions requirements identify JSAT–LAW 2026 and CLAT–PG 2026. This illustrates why applicants should decide at programme level rather than treating LSAT—India as a general Indian-law entrance option.
bullets: A historical LSAT—India result is an admissions credential only if a receiving institution currently confirms it will consider that result.
Current alternatives differ by university and degree programme; they are not interchangeable by default.
Any deadline, fee, score threshold, scholarship, or seat claim must be checked against the target institution's current official notice because such terms can change by intake.
